metadata.title: TMT Adaptive Optics Facility Status Report
metadata.description:
The TMT first-light adaptive optics (AO) facility comprises the Narrow Field InfraRed AO System (NFIRAOS), the

Laser Guide Star Facility (LGSF), and the AO Executive Software (AOESW). NFIRAOS is a 60x60-order laser guide

star (LGS) multi-conjugate AO (MCAO) system providing uniform, diffraction-limited performance in J, H, and K

bands over a 34 arcsecond diameter field with 50% sky coverage at the Galactic pole. Since the last reporting period,

substantial progress has been made across all subsystems. The NFIRAOS deformable mirror actuator lines are fully

fabricated and tested at CILAS, and the deformable mirror electronics final design has been completed at NRC. The real-

time controller (RTC) has surpassed 60 software releases and has been validated on-sky at 1 kHz with the NRC

REVOLT bench. The LGSF completed its first Final Design Review (FDR-1) in January 2026. The first-light

NFIRAOS-fed science instruments IRIS and MODHIS have passed their respective FDR-1 and Conceptual Design

Reviews, while WFOS completed its PDR-1. This paper summarizes the current status and highlights key developments

across AO systems, wavefront sensors, deformable mirrors, laser systems, instruments, and AO system engineering.

data.title: TMT Adaptive Optics Facility Status Report

metadata.title: knapsack-hld: Hybrid Lagrangian-Decomposition algorithm for the Selective Multiple-Choice Knapsack Problem
metadata.description:
Release accompanying the ITOR major-revision manuscript §4.8 (batch granularity, allocation error, and guarded deployment). Cited from the manuscript's Data & Code Availability Statement.

Changes since v1.1.0

  • Add guarded HLD — a never-lose deployment wrapper that returns max(Partition-Optimal, HLD) with a conservative Lagrangian skip, plus tests.
  • Add the batch-granularity study tooling: sweep runner, scripts/plot_batch_granularity.py, analysis/check scripts, and batch-granularity YAML configs under scripts/configs/.
  • CI green on Python 3.12 + 3.13 (ruff + pytest).

Reproducibility

Code concept DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20722604 resolves to the latest archived version. Benchmark instances are regenerated deterministically from the seed manifest; see the repository README.
metadata.notes: If you use this software, please cite both the software and the accompanying paper.

data.title: knapsack-hld: Hybrid Lagrangian-Decomposition algorithm for the Selective Multiple-Choice Knapsack Problem

metadata.title: GS1400/DG_Clarke: DG-Clarke Version 1.0.0
metadata.description:
Initial public release of DG-Clarke.

DG-Clarke is a MATLAB implementation of a derivative-free nonsmooth optimization method based on discrete-gradient approximations and MatCSG search directions. The solver is intended for unconstrained nonsmooth optimization problems where gradient or subgradient information is unavailable or impractical to obtain.

This release contains:

  • the main solver, DG_Clarke.m;
  • the driver file, driver_DG_Clarke.m;
  • the finite-max test environment in TE/TE.mat;
  • the objective evaluator TE/TE_realML_minmax_eval.m;
  • hitlist files for the 80 finite-max benchmark problems;
  • documentation and usage instructions in README.md.

The software accompanies the paper:

M. Kimiaei, S. Babaie-Kafaki, and Z. Aminifard, An Approximate Conjugate Subgradient Algorithm with Matrix Parameter for Derivative-Free Nonsmooth Optimization Problems, arXiv:2606.29084, 2026. https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29084

This project was funded by the Austrian Science Fund (FWF) under Grant DOI: 10.55776/PAT2747625.

metadata.title: Q-ROS: A MATLAB toolbox for the Quantum simulations of Random Open Systems
metadata.description:
This document describes the MATLAB toolbox “Q-ROS” (Quantum simulations of Random Open Systems), which is developed to simulate Markovian open quantum systems characterized by random-matrix configurations. The governing equation for the quantum simulation is the Lindblad quantum master equation, which is also known as the Gorini–Kossakowski–Sudarshan–Lindblad (GKSL) equation.

-        The codes include the characterization of random open quantum systems under random matrix theory (RMT).

-        The codes are developed with MATLAB R2023b.

If you use this code, or any part of it, in academic work, please cite the following paper and the archived software release:

[Yu, Sunkyu, Xianji Piao, and Namkyoo Park. "Heavy-tailed open quantum systems reveal long-lived and ultrasensitive coherence." arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.22717 (2025)] DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.2506.22717

Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.21104414

data.title: Q-ROS: A MATLAB toolbox for the Quantum simulations of Random Open Systems

metadata.title: Advances in the Instrument Control System Software Design for MORFEO: Integration with MICADO and the post-focal Deformable Mirrors in the ELT Environment
metadata.description:
MORFEO is the multi-conjugate adaptive optics (MCAO) module for the Extremely Large Telescope (ELT)

of ESO. It is conceived to provide diffraction-limited imaging over a wide field of view, serving MICADO, the

ELT’s first-light near-infrared imager and spectrograph, and supporting a future second-generation instrument

(HARMONI). The system is now progressing toward the Final Design Review with significant updates underway

in the architecture of its Instrument Control System Software (ICSS). This paper presents the latest updates

to the design of the MORFEO ICSS. Particular attention is paid to integration with the MICADO ICSS and

to control and monitoring of the DMs. When operating in MCAO mode, the Deformable Mirrors (DMs) are

controlled by the MORFEO Real-Time Computer. In contrast, in single-conjugate AO mode, the DM’s shape is

predefined and set by the MORFEO ICSS. MICADO and MORFEO are two independent instruments, but they

each need to use functions of the other system to be properly configured and used. Two interface libraries are

provided for this purpose. The first offers MICADO a way to use MORFEO functionalities at the observation

block level, in a totally transparent way, hiding the AO complexities. The second provides access to specific

MICADO functionalities needed for certain calibrations of the AO module. We discuss the adopted design

solutions and ongoing efforts to ensure robustness, modularity, and seamless integration within the ELT control

environment.

data.title: Advances in the Instrument Control System Software Design for MORFEO: Integration with MICADO and the post-focal Deformable Mirrors in the ELT Environment

files.key: Advances in the Instrument Control System Software Design - Elia Costa.pdf

metadata.title: Supplementary Software 1-3 and raw curvature data for Figure 5c in San Martin et al "Core–sheath coupling controls flagellar curvature and motility in Leptospira"
metadata.description:
Home-made scripts from: F San Martin, MR Brady, L Fule, L Nouchikian, A Rodriguez, M Duchateau, S Mondino, N Larrieux, EA Wunder Jr, AI Ko, M Rey, J Chamot-Rooke, R Duran, F Trajtenberg, M Picardeau, CV Sindelar, A Buschiazzo "Core–sheath coupling controls flagellar curvature and motility in Leptospira"
Nature Communications (2026). 

These document the methods referenced in the article. They are provided to show how the analyses were done (the cryo-EM raw data RELION star files and intermediate coordinate files are not part of this deposit).

The curvature data produced by script #2 ('02_crenshaw_curvature_torsion.sh') underlying Fig. 5c of the San Martin F et al article, are also hereby provided for each of the three Leptospira strains studied.

data.title: Supplementary Software 1-3 and raw curvature data for Figure 5c in San Martin et al "Core–sheath coupling controls flagellar curvature and motility in Leptospira"

metadata.title: Business and Technical Requirements of Software-As-A-Service: Implications in Portuguese Enterprise Business Context
metadata.description:
Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) is a viable option for some companies bearing their business processes. There is a considerable adoption rate, with companies already using more than two services for over two years. However, while some companies have plans to put more business processes supported by these services in the near future, others do not know if they will. They have several concerns regarding the software providers’ service level. These concerns are mainly technical and functional issues, service availability and payment models. There are major changes compared to the traditional software that have
implications on how the software is developed and made available to the users. The existing research addresses specific aspects and few studies give a broader view of the implications of SaaS for anyone who develops and provides software, and also for those who consumes it as an end user. What are the real needs of the Portuguese market? What fears and what is being done to mitigate them? Where should we focus our attention related to the SaaS offering in order to create more value? Thus, to analyze these questions four exploratory case studiesare used to assess the possible implications of SaaS on software
developers or software providers based in Portugal and also on end-users. This article appears in the context of a realistic and deep research that includes the involvement of managers, leaders and decision makers of Portuguese companies, to realize what actually constitutes a problem in SaaS and what effectively companies would like to have available in this offer. The results of this study reveal that SaaS effectively constitutes a very interesting and solid solution for the development of Portuguese companies, however there is a lack for greater efforts particularly in terms of customization for each customer (tenant) and integration with the back-end on-premise applications.

data.title: Business and Technical Requirements of Software-As-A-Service: Implications in Portuguese Enterprise Business Context

metadata.title: Towards a common SMP for NFDI
metadata.description:
Similar to Data Management Plans, Software Management Plans (SMPs) help researchers to manage their software based on good practices and the FAIR for Research Software principles. To do so, SMPs comprise a series of questions, including information about the software itself. A variety of SMPs exist, requiring a comparative analysis to establish a unified, up-to-date framework, tailored to the German National Research Data Infrastructures (NFDI) consortia. Following an initial comparison of current SMPs, a hackthon was held at ZB MED, focusing on harmonisation across the different SMPs, aligned to NFDI and RDMO needs. Here we present the results of this hackathon organised within the framework of the NFDI Base project DMP4NFDI. 

Acknowledgements
Angela Kranz is supported by DataPLANT, funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG), project number 442077441
Marco Reidelbach is supported by MaRDI, funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG), project number 460135501, NFDI 29/1 “MaRDI – Mathematische Forschungsdateninitiative”.
Laura Bahamón and David Walter are supported by the MAUS project, funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG), project number 543616919.
The DMP4NFDI service is developed as a service in Base4NFDI. Base4NFDI is funded by DFG, as part of NFDI, under project number 521453681. Bernd Müller, Marisabel Ocanto, Leyla Jael Castro, and Sabine Schönau are part of the core team of DMP4NFDI.
